title: Fabry-Perot Interference, g-factor Anisotropy, and Gate-Tunable Quantum dot in Chiral Tellurium Nanowires
authors: Suresh Ghimire, Mohammad Hafijur Rahaman, Nathan Tanner Sawyers, Madan Mohan Bhandari, Gokul Acharya, Syed Zulfiqar Hussain Shah, Iris Nandhakumar, Pawan Kumar, Zainul Aabdin Khan, Hugh O. H. Churchill, Dharmraj Kotekar-Patil
abstract: Chiral materials with strong spin-orbit coupling offer a unique platform for exploring the interplay between topology, chirality, and quantum transport yet the quantum coherent regime in elemental tellurium nanostructures remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate phase-coherent quasi-ballistic transport, anisotropic Zeeman spectroscopy, and gate-tunable quantum dot formation in hydrothermally grown t-tellurium nanowires. Single nanowire field-effect transistors exhibit p-type transport with hole mobilities rising from approx. 80 cm2 V-1 s-1 at 210 K to approx. 190 cm2 V-1 s-1 at 1 K, consistent with a crossover from phonon-limited to Coulomb scattering dominated regimes near 50 K. Notably, devices segregate into two distinct regimes based on their room temperature two-terminal resistance : low-resistance devices (< 30 kOhm) exhibit Fabry-Perot interference, whereas high resistance devices (> 30 kOhm) display Coulomb-blockade behavior revealing a two-terminal resistance-driven transition between quasi-ballistic and strongly localized transport regimes. Zeeman spectroscopy in in-plane and out-of-plane magnetic fields yields highly anisotropic Lande g-factors (an in-plane gparallel = 1.18 and an out-of-plane gperp = 18.41) and directly resolves a spin-orbit energy gap DeltaSO = 0.864 meV from an avoided crossing. These results establish chiral tellurium nanowires as a versatile platform for gate-defined spin qubits exploiting large, tunable g-factors and for hybrid tellurium-superconductor architectures targeting Majorana zero modes in an elemental vdW system.
summary: This work characterizes tellurium nanowires as promising platforms for quantum electronics by demonstrating phase-coherent transport regimes (Fabry-Perot interference and Coulomb blockade). Key findings include measuring large g-factor anisotropy and resolving an intrinsic spin-orbit coupling gap. These results position Te NWs for use in gate-defined spin qubits and topological superconducting research.
main_problem: To explore quantum coherent transport, g-factor anisotropy, and gate-tunable quantum dot formation in chiral tellurium nanowires.
main_result: The study demonstrated highly anisotropic Lande g-factors ($g_{\perp} \gg g_{\parallel}$) and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling ($\Delta_{SO}$), establishing Te NWs as a versatile platform for gate-defined spin qubits.
method: Electrical transport measurements, including temperature sweeps, Zeeman spectroscopy using magnetic fields, and analysis of Fabry-Perot interference versus Coulomb blockade regimes.
model_or_system: The physical system is hydrothermally grown tellurium (t-Te) nanowires, which are quasi-1D semiconductors exhibiting strong spin-orbit coupling due to their chiral structure. The measurements probe single-electron transistor behavior and coherent quantum transport phenomena.
key_observables: Hole mobility ($\mu$), Fabry-Perot interference oscillations, Coulomb blockade signatures, in-plane ($g_{\parallel}$) and out-of-plane ($g_{\perp}$) Landé g-factors, and the spin-orbit energy gap ($\Delta_{SO}$).
important_parameters: Temperature range (1 K to 210 K), resistance thresholds for transport regimes ($\le 30 	ext{ k}\Omega$ vs. $\ge 30 	ext{ k}\Omega$), and gate-tunable quantum dot size/charging energy.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed transport phenomena are dominated by single-particle physics, allowing for clear separation of quasi-ballistic (FP) and localized (CB) regimes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate NW morphology via STEM/SEM, temperature dependence of mobility showing scattering crossovers, conductance oscillations confirming FP interference in low-R devices, and peak splitting revealing g-factor anisotropy and SOC gap in magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing material quality (morphology, basic transport) to characterizing different quantum regimes: first, the crossover between quasi-ballistic (FP) and localized (CB) transport based on resistance; second, quantifying spin properties via Zeeman spectroscopy ($g$-factor anisotropy); and finally, demonstrating electrostatic tunability of the resulting quantum dot.
why_interesting: The combination of large, tunable g-factors and intrinsic SOC in an elemental vdW system makes this material highly relevant for solid-state qubits, offering a platform to study spin physics without relying on complex heterostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The measurement of large g-factor anisotropy ($g_{\perp} \gg g_{\parallel}$) and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling ($\Delta_{SO}$) directly probes the correlation between the electron's internal spin state and its external motion/orbital character within the nanowire.
